blocks.body: Defining Relative Clauses shows you how to add essential information that identifies exactly which person or thing you mean.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"Defining relative clauses give information that is necessary to understand exactly who or what we are talking about.","rules":[{"label":"Essential information","color":"emerald","text":"Without this clause, the noun becomes too general or unclear.","example":"Students who arrive late must wait outside."},{"label":"No commas","color":"blue","text":"In defining clauses, we normally do not use commas.","example":"The phone that I bought yesterday is already faulty."},{"label":"Common pronouns","color":"amber","text":"The most common words are who, which, that, and sometimes whose and where.","example":"The girl whose bike was stolen called the police."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. When we use a defining clause
blocks.body:
A defining clause is needed when it identifies the noun.

  • The people who work on weekends get extra pay. - not all people, only the ones who work at weekends.
  • The key that opens this door is missing. - we mean one specific key.

If you remove the clause, the listener may not know which person or thing you mean.

blocks.heading: 2. The usual form
blocks.body:
• who - for people: The man who lives upstairs is a chef.
  • which - for things: The laptop which I use for work is very old.
  • that - often for people and things in neutral style: The film that won the prize was excellent.
  • where - for places: The office where she works is near the station.
  • whose - for possession: The student whose notes I borrowed is absent.

blocks.heading: 3. How the meaning changes
blocks.body:
Compare these examples:

  • People who speak two languages often switch faster between tasks. - we mean only one group of people.
  • Books that explain grammar clearly are easier to revise from. - not all books, only a certain type.

A defining clause narrows the meaning of the noun. That is why commas are not normally used here.

blocks.heading: 4. Common mistakes
blocks.body:
• Mistake: adding commas as if the information were extra. In a defining clause, commas are usually wrong.
  • Mistake: using which for people. In standard learner English, who or that is better for people.
  • Mistake: missing the main meaning. If the clause answers which one? or which people?, it is probably defining.

blocks.heading: 5. Quick rule
blocks.body:
• A defining relative clause gives essential information.
  • These clauses are usually written without commas.
  • Choose who / which / that / whose / where according to the noun.
  • If the sentence becomes unclear without the clause, it is defining.
